title: Pineapple upside-down cake
subtitle: A caramelised pineapple top revealed when the cake is inverted
excerpt: A butter-cake baked with rings of pineapple and glacé cherries pressed into a caramel base, then turned out to show a glossy, sticky topping. Warm from the oven is best.
sourceNotes: Upside-down cakes cooked with fruit pressed into a caramelised base appear in American baking literature from the early twentieth century, shortly after canned pineapple became widely available. The pineapple upside-down cake is documented in US home-economics manuals and newspaper recipe columns from the 1920s onwards. Eliza Acton (Modern Cookery for Private Families, 1845, Project Gutenberg) describes the principle of a fruit-topped pudding inverted for service, which is the older British precedent.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of beating softened butter and sugar together until the mixture is pale, fluffy, and increased in volume. The air incorporated during creaming is what leavens a cake made by this method.

content.text: The appeal of this cake is the reveal: plain batter on top in the tin becomes a glossy, caramel-drenched pineapple topping when turned out. Work quickly once you've built the caramel base; the butter and sugar begin to set as they cool, and the pineapple rings need to be pressed in while the caramel is still fluid.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Melt 50 g butter in the base of the tin over low heat or in the oven, then scatter the brown sugar over evenly. Arrange the drained pineapple rings in a single layer on top of the butter and sugar — one ring in the centre, six around the edge. Place a glacé cherry in the centre of each ring. The caramel base is now ready for the cake batter.
Beat the softened butter and caster sugar together using the

until pale and fluffy, about 3 to 4 minutes with a hand mixer. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each. If the mixture curdles slightly, add a tablespoon of flour. Fold in the remaining flour and vanilla extract until just combined.

Spoon the batter carefully over the pineapple base, spreading it evenly without disturbing the fruit arrangement. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the top is golden brown and a skewer through the centre comes out clean. The sides should have pulled away from the tin.

Allow the cake to cool in the tin for exactly 5 minutes — long enough to begin to set, short enough that the caramel has not hardened against the tin. Place a plate larger than the tin over the top and flip firmly in one movement. Lift the tin away; the caramel will run over the pineapple. Serve warm. Any caramel left in the tin can be scraped out and poured over the top.
